Title: register_blueprint name override bypasses dot validation
Description: The `Blueprint` constructor explicitly prevents dots in names (`if "." in name: raise ValueError`) because dots denote nested blueprints. However, `app.register_blueprint(..., name="...")` doesn't enforce this check on the overridden name.

If a blueprint is registered with a dot via the `name` kwarg, it bypasses the restriction. This tricks the framework into treating it as a nested blueprint, causing the parent's callbacks (like `before_request`) to unexpectedly execute for the unrelated child blueprint.

### How to replicate the bug
```python
import flask

app = flask.Flask(__name__)
parent = flask.Blueprint("parent", __name__)
child = flask.Blueprint("child", __name__)

parent.before_request(lambda: setattr(flask.g, "hit", True))
child.add_url_rule("/", "index", lambda: str(hasattr(flask.g, "hit")))

app.register_blueprint(parent)
app.register_blueprint(child, name="parent.fake") # Bypasses dot validation
... [truncated]

Title: FLASK_DEBUG env variable no longer works as of click 8.4.0
Description: **Description of bug:**

FLASK_DEBUG=1 was working previously to set flask's debug mode either in a .env/.flaskenv and using python-dotenv OR by manually setting with export FLASK_DEBUG=1. As of today, setting it by env variable no longer works. Flask will still use debug mode using the --debug flag. Downgrading click to 8.3.3 resolves the issue.

**Replicate bug:**

```
python3 -m venv venv
. venv/bin/activate
pip install flask
cat << EOF > app.py
from flask import Flask
app = Flask(__name__)
@app.route('/')
def index():
    return 'hello world'
EOF
export FLASK_DEBUG=1
flask run
```

**This produces:**

Debug mode: off

```
pip install click==8.3.3
flask run
```

**Then produces:**

Debug mode: on

**Environment:**

- Python version: 3.12.3
- Flask version: 3.1.3

Title: Config.from_file() silently ignores fewer OSError codes than from_pyfile()
Description: ## Summary

`Config.from_file()` and `Config.from_pyfile()` behave inconsistently when `silent=True` and an `OSError` is raised.

`from_pyfile()` silences errors for these `errno` codes:

```python
if silent and e.errno in (errno.ENOENT, errno.EISDIR, errno.ENOTDIR):
    return False
```

But `from_file()` only silences:

```python
if silent and e.errno in (errno.ENOENT, errno.EISDIR):
    return False
```

It is missing `errno.ENOTDIR`.

## Reproduction

`ENOTDIR` is raised when a path component is a regular file, not a directory. For example, if `/tmp/myconfig` is a regular file and you try to open `/tmp/myconfig/settings.json`, the OS raises `ENOTDIR` (not a directory).

```python
import os, tempfile
from flask import Flask

app = Flask(__name__)

# create a file where we expect a directory
with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(delete=False, suffix="cfg") as f:
    f.write(b"")
    tmp = f.name... [truncated]

Title: fix: celery task result endpoint crashes on task failure
Description: ## Description

When a Celery task fails, `result.ready()` evaluates to `True` but `result.successful()` is `False`. 
Calling `result.get()` without `propagate=False` on a failed task will re-raise the task's exception, causing the Flask endpoint to crash with a 500 Internal Server Error instead of returning the task's failed status. 
Additionally, if `result.result` is an Exception object, returning it directly in the dictionary will cause a `TypeError` during JSON serialization.


**Severity**: `medium`
**File**: `examples/celery/src/task_app/views.py`

## Expected Behavior

The code should handle this case properly to avoid unexpected errors or degraded quality.

Title: Clarify recommended debug workflow in Quickstart documentation
Description: While reviewing the Quickstart documentation (https://flask.palletsprojects.com/en/stable/quickstart/#debug-mode), I noticed that debug mode is discussed but the distinction between using `app.run(debug=True)` and the recommended `flask run --debug` CLI flag is not clearly emphasized.

Many external tutorials still reference `app.run(debug=True)`, and new users may not understand when to use the CLI flag versus enabling debug mode directly in application code.

It may be helpful to clarify:

- That `flask run --debug` is the recommended development workflow
- When `app.run(debug=True)` is appropriate
- Why separating debug configuration from application code is considered best practice

Title: Add a built-in is_safe_redirect() helper to handle browser URL normalization (backslash → forward slash)
Description: Modern browsers normalize backslashes in navigation URLs (e.g., `\\evil.com`) into forward slashes (`//evil.com`). This creates a discrepancy between how Flask/Werkzeug evaluates redirect targets and how user agents ultimately interpret them.

A common pattern used by Flask developers to prevent protocol-relative redirects is:

```python
if not url.startswith("//"):
    return redirect(url)
```

However, the following payload bypasses this logic:

```
\\evil.com
```

Flask/Werkzeug evaluates this as a safe relative path (`startswith("//") == False`), while browsers normalize it to:

```
https://evil.com
```

and perform an external redirect. This normalization behavior is consistent across Chrome, Firefox, and Safari.

As Flask does not provide a built-in redirect validation helper, developers routinely implement ad-hoc string checks that do not account for brows... [truncated]

Title: Add configurable JSON parsing limits and preprocessing hook
Description: Description
Flask currently parses JSON input using json.loads() without providing an application-level mechanism to enforce payload size limits or preprocess raw JSON before deserialization.
This feature would introduce configuration settings such as:
•⁠  ⁠JSON_MAX_STRING_LENGTH – to reject JSON payloads exceeding a defined size before parsing.
•⁠  ⁠JSON_PREPROCESS_RAW – to allow an optional callable to preprocess raw JSON text before json.loads() is executed.

Example of the Problem This Would Resolve
If a client sends a very large JSON payload (for example, 15–20MB), Flask will attempt to parse it entirely in memory. This can lead to unnecessary memory consumption and CPU usage, especially in API-heavy environments. While infrastructure-level limits (e.g., reverse proxies) may mitigate this, application-level safeguards provide defense-in-depth and improve reliability.
Currently, this problem can only be... [truncated]

Title: `provide_automatic_options` is weird
Description: While dealing with trying to detect duplicate routes in https://github.com/pallets/werkzeug/issues/3105, I ran into an issue with Flask, which adds `OPTIONS` to every single rule by default.

I started looking into Flask's `provide_automatic_options` feature, and it's just weird.

- Why is there a `PROVIDE_AUTOMATIC_OPTIONS` config? What benefit is there to turning `OPTIONS` off at all, let alone configuring it for a given deployment.
- Flask adds `OPTIONS` to every route, then has a check that runs on dispatching _every_ request to see if the method was `OPTIONS` and `provide_automatic_options` was enabled for the rule. Performing this check for every request seems wasteful, as opposed to adding an actual rule and endpoint so that the routing that's already happening handles it.
    - But adding a rule is also sort of bad, as it results in a bunch of duplicate rules since Flask has no way to know what other rules have already be... [truncated]

Title: Support for all methods in @app.route
Description: By default, `@app.route` sets `methods` to support only `GET` requests and the [documentation](https://flask.palletsprojects.com/en/stable/api/#flask.Flask.route) suggests that the parameters are then passed to `Rule`, where `methods=None` allows all methods to be used. In reality, even explicit `None` is treated as a default value and gets overwritten by `["GET"]`. I suggest adding a way to allow all methods by explicitly passing `None`.

This feature would allow more direct control over `Rule` parameters when using `@app.route`, only using defaults when no value was provided. It is currently possible to work around the issue by creating the `Rule` directly, but it would be more convinient to have that option with `@app.route` decorators. It would also remove some confusion, as `None` being treated the same way as no value by `@app.route` while having a special meaning in `Rule` seems like a bug.

Title: Config.from_file() missing ENOTDIR in silent error handling
Description: ## Description

`Config.from_file()` is missing `errno.ENOTDIR` in its silent error handling, while `Config.from_pyfile()` correctly includes it.

In `from_pyfile` (line ~193):
```python
if silent and e.errno in (errno.ENOENT, errno.EISDIR, errno.ENOTDIR):
```

In `from_file` (line ~239):
```python
if silent and e.errno in (errno.ENOENT, errno.EISDIR):
```

The `ENOTDIR` case was added to `from_pyfile` in PR #2581 (fixing issue #2545), but the fix was never applied to `from_json` (now `from_file`), even though the original issue title mentioned "from_json, etc."

## Reproduction

When a component of the config file path is a regular file rather than a directory (e.g., `~/.myapp` is a file, and you try to load `~/.myapp/config.toml`), `from_file()` with `silent=True` raises `OSError` with `errno.ENOTDIR` instead of silently returning `False`.

```python
import flask
import tomllib

app = flask.Flask(__name__... [truncated]

Title: Internal refactor: simplify response tuple unpacking in make_response in app.py
Description: This is an internal, non-user-facing refactor.

The `make_response` method currently contains inline logic to unpack
response tuples with different valid shapes. While this logic is correct,
it is dense and increases cognitive load in an already large method.

Refactoring this logic into a small private helper would improve
readability and long-term maintainability without changing behavior.

The current tuple unpacking logic handles multiple valid forms
((body, status, headers), (body, status), (body, headers)) inline inside
`make_response`. This makes the control flow harder to follow and
duplicates validation responsibilities within the method.

This is not easily improved via subclassing or extensions, as the logic
is internal to Flask's response handling. A small internal refactor would
isolate this responsibility and make future maintenance safer.

Title: package detection fails with namespace package in editable mode
Description: Given a package which uses namespace packaging and has been installed using "editable installs", Flask's scaffold (specifically `_find_package_path`) will error if the name passed to `Flask(...)` is the package name rather than a sub-module.

This affects cases where the name is the name of the package (and thus potentially not actually the `__name__`) though also happens when using `__name__` and the app is defined within a `__init__.py` in the root of the namespaced package.

Title: change default redirect code to 303
Description: Flask and Werkzeug `redirect` currently defaults to a [302](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Reference/Status/302). Routing uses [307](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Reference/Status/307) since that preserves method consistently. We didn't change the `redirect` default to 307, since that would break the common pattern of "GET form, POST form, redirect to GET result", ending up doing "POST result" instead. [303](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Reference/Status/303) seems designed exactly for this pattern, so that a redirect always results in a GET, instead of preserving the method or only rewriting it sometimes. [HTMX actually calls this out about Flask.](https://hypermedia.systems/htmx-patterns/#:~:text=cleaner%2E-,A,resource)

I don't _think_ there would actually be a problem switching to 303 as the default. It would still do the expected thing for basic page redirects and for... [truncated]

Title: Add explicit support for async teardown_request handlers
Description: ### Summary

At the moment, `teardown_request` handlers in Flask are synchronous and cannot be defined as async functions. This makes it harder for apps that depend on async cleanup logic (e.g., closing async DB connections or awaiting async tasks) to integrate cleanly with Flask’s request lifecycle.

### Problem

When users attempt to define an async teardown handler like:

```python
@app.teardown_request
async def cleanup_async_resources(response):
    await close_async_connections()
```

Flask does not await it and treats it as a regular function, leading to unawaited coroutine warnings and the async cleanup not running as intended.

### Proposed Solution
Make teardown handlers awaitable - if a handler is declared async, Flask should await it during the teardown phase.

### Example
```python
@app.teardown_request
async def cleanup_async_resources(response):
    await some_async_cleanup()  # should be awaite... [truncated]

Title: asgiref fails with gevent patching
Description: When `gevent.monkey.patch_all()` is used, `async def` functions fail when multiple concurrent requests are sent. asgiref gives the error `RuntimeError: You cannot use AsyncToSync in the same thread as an async event loop - just await the async function directly.`. This suggests that somehow, gevent is causing a running loop to be visible across threads.

`example.py`:

```python
# /// script
# requires-python = "~=3.14.0"
# dependencies = [
#     "flask[async]",
#     "gevent",
# ]
#
# [tool.uv]
# exclude-newer = "2026-01-05T00:00:00Z"
#
# [tool.uv.sources]
# flask = { path = ".", editable = true }

Title: Teardown handler chain exception handling
Description: Flask executes registered teardown handlers (used for closing DB connections, releasing locks, etc.) in a chain. The do_teardown_request method which is supposed to make these teardown requests does not wrap individual handlers in a try/except block. If one handler raises an exception, the loop terminates immediately, and all subsequent handlers are skipped, this can lead to the application to skip critical cleanup routines if a non critical one fails.

@app.route('/')
def index():
    global mock_db_connections
    mock_db_connections = mock_db_connections + 1
    return "Request Processed"

#simulating a critical teardown handler which is supposed to free resources from the db

Title: Modern CSRF Protection Using `Sec-Fetch-Site` Header
Description: Flask's documentation states that CSRF protection requires a form validation framework (which Flask doesn't provide), necessitating one-time tokens stored in cookies and transmitted with form data. However, modern browsers now support `Sec-Fetch-Site` headers, making token-based CSRF protection unnecessary. Rails just merged this approach in [rails/rails#56350](https://github.com/rails/rails/pull/56350). Flask should offer a similar modern solution—but in a more Flask-like way: as a simple argument to `@app.route()`.

## Current State

From Flask's security documentation:

> "Why does Flask not do that for you? The ideal place for this to happen is the form validation framework, which does not exist in Flask."

This was written when CSRF tokens were the only viable protection mechanism. That's no longer true. And with header-based protection, no form validation framework is needed—just a simple check before dispat... [truncated]

Title: deprecate `should_ignore_error`
Description: This was added in f1918093ac70d589a4d67af0d77140734c06c13d as part of the original code to keep the context around for use in the debugger, tests, etc. It was not part of a PR, and there's no linked issue or explanation on why it was added.

The intention seems to be to allow ignoring certain errors during debugging, so that cleanup is still run immediately. That's not how context preservation works anymore.  It also causes the exception to not be passed to teardown handlers, but there doesn't seem to be any reason to hide that, and handlers can already choose what to do if they're passed an error.

The method is only documented in the API, not in any other pages. There's no test for it. I have a feeling this isn't used. It results in an extra function call every single request, only to always return false. This can be deprecated then removed.

Title: `flask.testing` misleadingly appears to import `TestResponse`
Description: `testing.py` contains the following:
```python
if t.TYPE_CHECKING:  # pragma: no cover
    from werkzeug.test import TestResponse
```
As such, any IDE or static checker will consider this to be valid code:
```python
import flask.testing as _flask_test

def my_test_helper(flask_test_client: _flask_test.FlaskClient) -> _flask_test.TestResponse:
    ...
```

However, at runtime, this code will fail, because `TestResponse` is not actually imported into the `flask.testing` namespace.

Possible solutions:
- Move `from werkzeug.test import TestResponse` outside the `if t.TYPE_CHECKING` block; you're already importing `werkzeug.test` in that file anyway
- Remove `from werkzeug.test import TestResponse` entirely and replace `TestResponse` with `werkzeug.test.TestResponse` in type-hints
- Change `from werkzeug.test import TestResponse` to `from werkzeug.test import TestResponse as _TestResponse`; the leading unders... [truncated]

Title: document that `request.full_path` leaves query string percent encoded
Description: WHAT IS THE BUG?

`request.full_path` is documented as *“Requested path as unicode, including the query string.”*

Title: The `template_filter` decorator doesn't work if you don't pass an argument
Description: ## What's the issue?

You can use `template_filter` as a decorator, but it only registers the filter if you write an explicit name or an empty set of parentheses. If you call it without parens, the filter doesn't get registered.

It's a small difference and can be confusing.

## Minimal example

Consider the following program:

```python
from flask import Flask, render_template_string


app = Flask(__name__)


@app.template_filter
def double(x):
    return x * 2


@app.route("/")
def index():
    return render_template_string("2 times 2 is {{ 2 | double }}")
```

If you run this app (`flask run --port 8008 --debug`) and then open it in your browser (`http://localhost:8008`) you'll get an error:

```
jinja2.exceptions.TemplateAssertionError: No filter named 'double'.
```

This is confusing, and it took me a while to realise the missing parentheses in `app.template_filter` were at fault.

Title: Recommend Warning and Safer Defaults for url_for(..., _external=True)
Description: Hi Flask team,

We recently analyzed several Flask-based applications and noticed a recurring security concern related to url_for(..., _external=True) when used in untrusted request contexts. Specifically, since it uses request.host by default to construct the external URL, applications that do not explicitly configure SERVER_NAME or sanitize headers can be vulnerable to host header injection.

To improve developer awareness and reduce misuse, we suggest: Add a warning to the url_for documentation about the risk of relying on request.host, and recommend the use of SERVER_NAME or trusted_hosts when generating external URLs.

We’d be happy to help draft the relevant documentation or contribute a pull request if this direction aligns with the maintainers' goals.

Best regards,
Rui Yang and Zhengyu Liu
Johns Hopkins University

Title: Reference cycle caused by exception handling
Description: A reference cycle is introduced by Flask's exception handling mechanism. Specifically, the exception object retains a reference to the stack frame, which in turn holds a reference back to the exception. This creates a reference cycle between the traceback and the exception instance.

This becomes particularly problematic in applications where the garbage collector is disabled (gc.disable()). Even when the GC is enabled, this cycle puts unnecessary pressure on memory management.

The issue originates from the following line in the Flask source code: https://github.com/pallets/flask/blob/9225f8bb28d291b1610c8a58e3233aa6bbdc1808/src/flask/app.py#L1507

The fix is simple: the reference to the exception object should be explicitly released once it is no longer needed.

A minimal reproducible example demonstrating this issue can be found here:
👉 https://github.com/tomasz-pankowski/exception-memory-leak/tree/main

Title: `prepare_body` stream detection regression
Description: <!-- Summary. -->
Requests 2.34.0 changed the stream detection in PreparedRequest.prepare_body() from `hasattr(data, "__iter__")` to `isinstance(data, Iterable)`. This breaks file-like wrappers that expose `__iter__/read/tell/seek` via `__getattr__ delegation` (e.g. tqdm.wrapattr), because `isinstance(..., Iterable)` only checks the class MRO, not dynamic attribute delegation. As a result, `_body_position` is never recorded and the body cannot be rewound on 307/308 redirects.
## Expected Result

Upload completes successfully through the redirect, as it did in 2.33.1.

## Actual Result

The redirected request sends an empty/consumed body. The server receives no data and the request times out:
```
requests.exceptions.ReadTimeout: HTTPSConnectionPool(...): Read timed out. (read timeout=30)
```

import requests
from tqdm import tqdm

with open("testfile.txt", "wb") as f:

Title: [Docs] Sidebar is hidden in mobile layout
Description: ## Problem Description

Theoretically, for documentation using the [alabaster](https://github.com/sphinx-doc/alabaster) theme, the sidebar content should appear at either the **very top** or the **very bottom** in a mobile layout. For example:

- https://alabaster.readthedocs.io/
- https://docs.kernel.org/
- https://www.writethedocs.org/

However, I noticed that the current Requests documentation fails to display sidebar content in its mobile layout.

Based on a preliminary investigation, this issue seems to be caused by modifications to `sphinxsidebar` within the current `custom.css`:

https://github.com/psf/requests/blob/4b0b1a3e9f2fc21b9dcd8b906f2ff02645aa697e/docs/_static/custom.css#L9-L13

The simplest fix is to delete the following line:

```
  div.sphinxsidebar {display: none;}
```

For the most downloaded Python package in history, this is worth saying: Requests is exceptionally well-maintained. Our full scan at severity LOW produced only code style findings (complexity, function length) beyond this single reliability observation. Zero security findings in core code.

Title: fish completion is broken in 8.4.1
Description: Commit 19fd4d6 (#3126) broke fish completion.

## Unquoted `\n`

```diff
-        set -l metadata (string split "," $completion);
+        set -l metadata (string split \n $completion);
```
Without quotes, `\n` is evaluated as a literal newline, which splits the command across lines in the generated script:
```
        set -l metadata (string split
 $completion);
```

## Command substitution flattens newlines

Even if quoted, `\n` fails as a separator. The Python side outputs:
```python
        return f"{item.type}\n{value}\n{help_escaped}"
```
But when captured via command substitution in Fish, the newlines are flattened:
```fish
    set -l response (env %(complete_var)s=fish_complete COMP_WORDS=(commandline -cp) \
COMP_CWORD=(commandline -t) %(prog_name)s);
```

---

To support multi-line, maybe we just need to escape `\n` in help message while keeping the old separator? Not 100% sure.

Either way, we should probably revert this ... [truncated]

Title: FloatRange accepts NaN, bypassing range validation
Description: ## Bug

`click.FloatRange` accepts `NaN` as a valid value, bypassing range validation entirely. In contrast, `inf` and `-inf` are correctly rejected.

## Reproduction

```python
import click
from click.testing import CliRunner

@click.command()
@click.option("--temperature", type=click.FloatRange(-50.0, 150.0))
def cmd(temperature):
    click.echo(f"Processing: {temperature}C")

runner = CliRunner()

# NaN passes validation (bug)
result = runner.invoke(cmd, ["--temperature", "nan"])
print(result.exit_code)  # 0 — should be 2
print(result.output)     # "Processing: nanC"

# inf is correctly rejected
result = runner.invoke(cmd, ["--temperature", "inf"])
print(result.exit_code)  # 2 — correct
```

## Root cause

`_NumberRangeBase.convert` checks `rv < min` and `rv > max`. Since `NaN < 0.0` and `NaN > 1.0` both evaluate to `False`, the check `lt_min or gt_max` is `False`, so NaN passes through.

Title: Support for custom plug-ins on playbooks under same directory structure as for collections
Description: ### Summary

Today, in a playbook directory, custom plug-ins must go under a folder called `<plug-in type>_plugins/`, like `filter_plugins/` or `lookup_plugins/`.
To add a custom plug-in to a collection, though, the plug-ins go under a folder `plugins/<plug-in type>`.
My idea is to add support so plug-ins in a playbook directory can also be added to `plugins/< plug-in type>` folders. Second, it will keep a standard "plug-ins under `plugins/` folder" idea. Of course, we must not disable the older path support, so it's an addition. Title: wait_for state=present aborts on first poll on z/OS USS due to hard-coded Linux ENOENT=2 check
Description: ### Summary

ansible.builtin.wait_for with path and state: present is intended to poll until a file appears, but on z/OS UNIX System Services (USS) it fails on the very first poll with Failed to stat <path>, EDC5129I No such file or directory. instead of waiting. The cause is a hard-coded comparison against Linux's ENOENT value (errno == 2); on z/OS USS, ENOENT is errno == 129, so the "file is not there yet, keep polling" branch is never taken and any not-yet-existing file is treated as a fatal stat error. The module only ever succeeds when the file already exists at the first check — it is functionally unusable as a "wait until file is created" primitive on z/OS.

Title: apt_repository_module is incompatible with modern debian sources format
Description: ### Summary

The built-in module `apt_repository` will create a `.list` file in `/etc/apt/sources.list.d/`. However on recent Ubuntu/Debian systems the command

`add-apt-repository ppa:mozillateam/ppa`

will create a `.sources` file in the same directory. That means the manual command and the module are incompatible. The `.sources` format is now recommended since trixie and will eventually be deprecated.

https://wiki.debian.org/SourcesList#debian.sources_format

There are a few possibilities to guarantee backwards compatibility:

- Use `apt modernize-sources` as part of the module
- Include a script to modernize the sources directly in the module
- Put the `.sources` format behind a feature flag
- Split the new format into a dedicated module (eg. `apt_repository_source`)

Title: Allow to reject (or warn) about extra values in `AnsiblePlugin.set_options()`'s `direct` parameter
Description: ### Summary

One long-standing and common problem of all lookup and inventory plugins is that if you provide an unknown option to the lookup call or inventory config file, it is simply ignored without any feedback. If a user makes a typo, or [misunderstands parameter names like `_terms` in lookup documentation](https://github.com/ansible-collections/community.general/issues/1943), there is no warning at all about this. Ansible should error out on superfluous parameters, or at least warn about them.

To avoid breaking backwards compatibility, this should be something that plugins explicitly request (even though I believe that 99.9% of all lookup and inventory plugins want a warning or error), as some lookups or inventory plugins require the current behavior to interpret arbitrary non-documented parameters. I think that plugins should also be able to chose between warnin... [truncated]

Title: sshpass password mechanism marks host UNREACHABLE when remote command exits non-zero
Description: ### Summary

When ANSIBLE_SSH_PASSWORD_MECHANISM=sshpass is set, any task that exits with a non-zero return code causes the host to be marked UNREACHABLE and fail. `ignore_errors: true`, `failed_when` do not help. 

What makes matters super confusing is that on `devel` this entire codepath appears to be deadcode: https://github.com/ansible/ansible/blob/devel/lib/ansible/plugins/connection/ssh.py#L493

There were some logging regressions that happened that caused this to never be called because `sshpass` is not included in the `cmd` that is being passed here. 

## Root cause

`_handle_error()` in `plugins/connection/ssh.py` treats sshpass exit codes 1–4 and 6 as connection failures and raises AnsibleConnectionFailure. Any failure in a `shell` or `command` task will catch this codepath and trigger the AnsibleConnectionFailure because fail_json will always cause an exit code of 1.

Downstream projects work around it today, e.g. kubespray coerces `ansible_os_family` to `RedHat` via `set_fact`:

- https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/kubespray/blob/master/roles/kubernetes/preinstall/tasks/0020-set_facts.yml

This workaround is more painful as injected `ansible_*` fact vars are phased out, since `set_fact` no longer updates `ansible_facts['os_family']`.

Scope: openEuler-based **server** edition only. Desktop UOS (Debian/Deepin-based) is unaffected and stays under the `Debian` family as `Uos`.

It is typically used for roles that define additional features on top of the `main.yml`, and allows to implement conditional logic without using variables.

Title: Deprecate ansible-pull CLI
Description: ### Summary

We've discussed and agreed within the core team to deprecate the `ansible-pull` CLI.

This CLI attempts to meet everyones needs and workflows. In doing so it has become overly complicated and comes with more caveats than it should.

Ultimately, the tool is extremely unnecessary, and users would be far better served with a few line shell script, or playbook. At the lowest level, `ansible-pull` is just calling `ansible` with `-m git` and then calling `ansible-playbook`. It does so in over 400 lines, a lot of which is complex argument parsing, and argument mapping.

I've put together some examples that are still varying levels of complexity that users can crib off of, that meet their needs much more succinctly. These examples can currently be found at https://github.com/sivel/ansible-pull

As part of this deprecation work, we should invest some time into documentation to explain and maybe give examples of the alternative workflow... [truncated]

Title: SimpleHttpServer survives ansible-galaxy test cleanup
Description: @ansibot bot_skip

The SimpleHttpServer started by the `ansible-galaxy` integration test needs to be explicitly shut down during cleanup. Automatic re-run of the test after, e.g., [a connection reset by public galaxy](https://dev.azure.com/ansible/ansible/_build/results?buildId=177871&view=logs&jobId=164833a7-901c-5488-2f1a-ebdcb41becbd&j=164833a7-901c-5488-2f1a-ebdcb41becbd&t=64ffd6e5-07be-5c47-b28b-80aed422f325) silently fails to re-launch the server (since the port is occupied by the still-running first instance) and reuses the original server running under an `async: 120` process that will die unexpectedly during the test run.

The port `wait_for` task used by the launch should also have a reasonable timeout (maybe 20-30s max)- if something went wrong with the server launch, it'd hang until the CI job timeout.
